title: Video game graphics

| Types  * [2.5D & 3/4 perspective](/wiki/2.5D "2.5D") * [First-person view](/wiki/First-person_(video_games) "First-person (video games)") * [Fixed 3D](/wiki/Fixed_3D "Fixed 3D") * [Full motion video based game](/wiki/Full_motion_video_based_game "Full motion video based game") * [Graphic adventure game](/wiki/Graphic_adventure_game "Graphic adventure game") * [Isometric video game graphics](/wiki/Isometric_video_game_graphics "Isometric video game graphics") * [Side-scrolling video game](/wiki/Side-scrolling_video_game "Side-scrolling video game") * [Stereoscopic video game](/wiki/Stereoscopic_video_game "Stereoscopic video game") * [Text-based game](/wiki/Text-based_game "Text-based game") * [Third-person view](/wiki/Third-person_view "Third-person view") * [Tile-based video game](/wiki/Tile-based_video_game "Tile-based video game") * [Top-down perspective](/wiki/Top-down_perspective "Top-down perspective") * [Vector game](/wiki/Vector_game "Vector game")

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Art_and_video_games "Template:Art and video games") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Art_and_video_games "Template talk:Art and video games") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Art_and_video_games "Special:EditPage/Template:Art and video games")

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Video+game+graphics%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Video+game+graphics%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Video+game+graphics%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Video+game+graphics%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(August 2010)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

or **text-based games** that used [text characters](/wiki/Character_set "Character set") instead of [bitmapped](/wiki/Bitmap "Bitmap") or [vector graphics](/wiki/Vector_graphics "Vector graphics"). Examples include [MUDs](/wiki/Multi-user_dungeon "Multi-user dungeon") (*multi-user dungeons*), where players could read or view depictions of rooms, objects, other players, and actions performed in the virtual world; and [roguelikes](/wiki/Roguelike "Roguelike"), a subgenre of [role-playing video games](/wiki/Role-playing_game_(video_games) "Role-playing game (video games)") featuring many monsters, items, and environmental effects, as well as an emphasis on [randomization](/wiki/Procedural_generation "Procedural generation"), replayability and [permanent death](/wiki/Permanent_death "Permanent death"). Some of the earliest text games were developed for computer systems which had no video display at all.

refer to the use of geometrical [primitives](/wiki/Primitive_(geometry) "Primitive (geometry)") such as [points](/wiki/Point_(spatial) "Point (spatial)"), [lines](/wiki/Line_(mathematics) "Line (mathematics)"), and [curves](/wiki/Curve "Curve") (i.e., shapes based on mathematical equations) instead of resolution-dependent bitmap graphics to represent [images](/wiki/Image "Image") in [computer graphics](/wiki/Computer_graphics "Computer graphics"). In video games this type of projection is somewhat rare, but has become more common in recent years in [browser-based gaming](/wiki/Browser_game "Browser game") with the advent of [Flash](/wiki/Adobe_Flash "Adobe Flash") and [HTML5 Canvas](/wiki/Canvas_element "Canvas element"), as these support vector graphics natively. An earlier example for the personal computer is *[Starglider](/wiki/Starglider "Starglider")* (1986).

can also refer to a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") that uses a [vector graphics display](/wiki/Vector_monitor "Vector monitor") capable of projecting images using an electron beam to draw images instead of with [pixels](/wiki/Pixel "Pixel"), much like a [laser show](/wiki/Laser_lighting_display "Laser lighting display"). Many early [arcade games](/wiki/Arcade_games "Arcade games") used such displays, as they were capable of displaying more detailed images than [raster displays](/wiki/Raster_graphics "Raster graphics") on the hardware available at that time. Many vector-based arcade games used full-color overlays to complement the otherwise monochrome vector images. Other uses of these overlays were very detailed drawings of the static gaming environment, while the moving objects were drawn by the vector beam. Games of this type were produced mainly by [Atari](/wiki/Atari "Atari"), [Cinematronics](/wiki/Cinematronics "Cinematronics"), and [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega"). Examples of vector games include *[Asteroids](/wiki/Asteroids_(video_game) "Asteroids (video game)")*, *[Armor Attack](/wiki/Armor_Attack "Armor Attack")*, *[Aztarac](/wiki/Aztarac "Aztarac")*, *[Eliminator](/wiki/Eliminator_(1981_video_game) "Eliminator (1981 video game)")*, *[Lunar Lander](/wiki/Lunar_Lander_(arcade_game) "Lunar Lander (arcade game)")*, *[Space Fury](/wiki/Space_Fury "Space Fury")*, *[Space Wars](/wiki/Space_Wars "Space Wars")*, *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(arcade_game) "Star Trek (arcade game)")*, *[Tac/Scan](/wiki/Tac/Scan "Tac/Scan")*, *[Tempest](/wiki/Tempest_(arcade_game) "Tempest (arcade game)")* and *[Zektor](/wiki/Zektor "Zektor")*. The [Vectrex](/wiki/Vectrex "Vectrex") [home console](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") also used a vector display. After 1985, the use of vector graphics declined substantially due to improvements in sprite technology; rasterized 3D Filled Polygon Graphics returned to the arcades and were so popular that vector graphics could no longer compete.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

A variety of [computer graphic](/wiki/Computer_graphics "Computer graphics") techniques have been used to display [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") content throughout the [history of video games](/wiki/History_of_video_games "History of video games"). The predominance of individual techniques have evolved over time, primarily due to [hardware](/wiki/Graphics_hardware "Graphics hardware") advances and restrictions such as the processing power of [central](/wiki/Central_processing_unit "Central processing unit") or [graphics processing units](/wiki/Graphics_processing_unit "Graphics processing unit").

## Text-based

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Text-based")]

Main article: [Text-based game](/wiki/Text-based_game "Text-based game")

Some of the earliest video games were **text games** or **text-based games** that used [text characters](/wiki/Character_set "Character set") instead of [bitmapped](/wiki/Bitmap "Bitmap") or [vector graphics](/wiki/Vector_graphics "Vector graphics"). Examples include [MUDs](/wiki/Multi-user_dungeon "Multi-user dungeon") (*multi-user dungeons*), where players could read or view depictions of rooms, objects, other players, and actions performed in the virtual world; and [roguelikes](/wiki/Roguelike "Roguelike"), a subgenre of [role-playing video games](/wiki/Role-playing_game_(video_games) "Role-playing game (video games)") featuring many monsters, items, and environmental effects, as well as an emphasis on [randomization](/wiki/Procedural_generation "Procedural generation"), replayability and [permanent death](/wiki/Permanent_death "Permanent death"). Some of the earliest text games were developed for computer systems which had no video display at all.

Text games are typically easier to write and require less processing power than [graphical games](/wiki/Graphical_games "Graphical games"), and thus were more common from 1970 to 1990. However, [terminal emulators](/wiki/Terminal_emulator "Terminal emulator") are still in use today, and people continue to play MUDs and explore [interactive fiction](/wiki/Interactive_fiction "Interactive fiction"). Many beginning programmers still create these types of games to familiarize themselves with a [programming language](/wiki/Programming_language "Programming language"), and contests are still held even today on who can finish programming a roguelike within a short time period, such as seven days.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Vector graphics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Vector graphics")]

Main article: [Vector graphics](/wiki/Vector_graphics "Vector graphics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Space_Rocks_%28game%29.jpg/250px-Space_Rocks_%28game%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Space_Rocks_(game).jpg)

*[Asteroids](/wiki/Asteroids_(video_game) "Asteroids (video game)")*-clone played on an [oscillograph](/wiki/Oscillograph "Oscillograph") configured as a vector display

**Vector graphics** refer to the use of geometrical [primitives](/wiki/Primitive_(geometry) "Primitive (geometry)") such as [points](/wiki/Point_(spatial) "Point (spatial)"), [lines](/wiki/Line_(mathematics) "Line (mathematics)"), and [curves](/wiki/Curve "Curve") (i.e., shapes based on mathematical equations) instead of resolution-dependent bitmap graphics to represent [images](/wiki/Image "Image") in [computer graphics](/wiki/Computer_graphics "Computer graphics"). In video games this type of projection is somewhat rare, but has become more common in recent years in [browser-based gaming](/wiki/Browser_game "Browser game") with the advent of [Flash](/wiki/Adobe_Flash "Adobe Flash") and [HTML5 Canvas](/wiki/Canvas_element "Canvas element"), as these support vector graphics natively. An earlier example for the personal computer is *[Starglider](/wiki/Starglider "Starglider")* (1986).

**Vector game** can also refer to a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") that uses a [vector graphics display](/wiki/Vector_monitor "Vector monitor") capable of projecting images using an electron beam to draw images instead of with [pixels](/wiki/Pixel "Pixel"), much like a [laser show](/wiki/Laser_lighting_display "Laser lighting display"). Many early [arcade games](/wiki/Arcade_games "Arcade games") used such displays, as they were capable of displaying more detailed images than [raster displays](/wiki/Raster_graphics "Raster graphics") on the hardware available at that time. Many vector-based arcade games used full-color overlays to complement the otherwise monochrome vector images. Other uses of these overlays were very detailed drawings of the static gaming environment, while the moving objects were drawn by the vector beam. Games of this type were produced mainly by [Atari](/wiki/Atari "Atari"), [Cinematronics](/wiki/Cinematronics "Cinematronics"), and [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega"). Examples of vector games include *[Asteroids](/wiki/Asteroids_(video_game) "Asteroids (video game)")*, *[Armor Attack](/wiki/Armor_Attack "Armor Attack")*, *[Aztarac](/wiki/Aztarac "Aztarac")*, *[Eliminator](/wiki/Eliminator_(1981_video_game) "Eliminator (1981 video game)")*, *[Lunar Lander](/wiki/Lunar_Lander_(arcade_game) "Lunar Lander (arcade game)")*, *[Space Fury](/wiki/Space_Fury "Space Fury")*, *[Space Wars](/wiki/Space_Wars "Space Wars")*, *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(arcade_game) "Star Trek (arcade game)")*, *[Tac/Scan](/wiki/Tac/Scan "Tac/Scan")*, *[Tempest](/wiki/Tempest_(arcade_game) "Tempest (arcade game)")* and *[Zektor](/wiki/Zektor "Zektor")*. The [Vectrex](/wiki/Vectrex "Vectrex") [home console](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") also used a vector display. After 1985, the use of vector graphics declined substantially due to improvements in sprite technology; rasterized 3D Filled Polygon Graphics returned to the arcades and were so popular that vector graphics could no longer compete.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Full motion video

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Full motion video")]

Main articles: [Full motion video](/wiki/Full_motion_video "Full motion video") and [Interactive film](/wiki/Interactive_film "Interactive film")

**Full motion video** (**FMV**) games are [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") that rely upon pre-recorded [television](/wiki/Television "Television")- or [movie](/wiki/Film "Film")-quality recordings and [animations](/wiki/Animation "Animation") rather than [sprites](/wiki/Sprite_(computer_graphics) "Sprite (computer graphics)"), [vectors](/wiki/Vector_graphics "Vector graphics") or 3D models to display action in the game. FMV-based games were popular during the early 1990s as [CD-ROMs](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM") and [Laserdiscs](/wiki/Laserdisc "Laserdisc") made their way into the living rooms, providing an alternative to the low-capacity [ROM cartridges](/wiki/ROM_cartridge "ROM cartridge") of most [consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") at the time. Although FMV-based games did manage to look better than many contemporary sprite-based games, they occupied a niche market; and a vast majority of FMV games were panned at the time of their release, with many gamers citing their dislike for the lack of interaction inherent in these games. As a result, the format became a [well-known failure in video gaming](/wiki/List_of_commercial_failures_in_video_gaming "List of commercial failures in video gaming"), and the popularity of FMV games declined substantially after 1995 as more advanced consoles started to become widely available.

A number of different types of games utilized this format. Some resembled modern [music/dance games](/wiki/Music_video_game "Music video game"), where the player timely presses buttons according to a screen instruction. Others included early [rail shooters](/wiki/Rail_shooter "Rail shooter") such as *[Tomcat Alley](/wiki/Tomcat_Alley "Tomcat Alley")*, *[Surgical Strike](/wiki/Surgical_Strike_(video_game) "Surgical Strike (video game)")* and *[Sewer Shark](/wiki/Sewer_Shark "Sewer Shark")*. Full motion video was also used in several [interactive movie](/wiki/Interactive_movie "Interactive movie") [adventure games](/wiki/Adventure_game "Adventure game"), such as *[The Beast Within: A Gabriel Knight Mystery](/wiki/The_Beast_Within:_A_Gabriel_Knight_Mystery "The Beast Within: A Gabriel Knight Mystery")* and *[Phantasmagoria](/wiki/Phantasmagoria_(video_game) "Phantasmagoria (video game)")*.

## 2D

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2D")]

See also: [2D computer graphics](/wiki/2D_computer_graphics "2D computer graphics")

Games utilizing [parallel projection](/wiki/Parallel_projection "Parallel projection") typically make use of two-dimensional [bitmap graphics](/wiki/Bitmap_graphics "Bitmap graphics") as opposed to 3D-rendered [triangle-based geometry](/wiki/Polygon_mesh "Polygon mesh"), allowing developers to create large, complex gameworlds efficiently and with relatively few art assets by dividing the art into sprites or [tiles](/wiki/Tile-based_video_game "Tile-based video game") and reusing them repeatedly (though some games use a mix of different techniques).

### Top-down perspective

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Top-down perspective")]

See also: [Bird's-eye view](/wiki/Bird%27s-eye_view "Bird's-eye view")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/TheHeist2_Robbery.png/250px-TheHeist2_Robbery.png)](/wiki/File:TheHeist2_Robbery.png)

An example of a typical top-down, third-person view game, *The Heist 2*

**Top-down perspective**, also sometimes referred to as **bird's-eye view**, **Overworld**, **Godview**, **overhead view**, or **helicopter view**, when used in the context of video games, refers to a camera angle that shows players and the areas around them from above. While not exclusive to video games utilizing parallel projection, it was at one time common among [2D](/wiki/2D_computer_graphics "2D computer graphics") [role playing](/wiki/Role-playing_game_(video_games) "Role-playing game (video games)") video games, [wargames](/wiki/Wargame_(video_games) "Wargame (video games)"), and [construction and management simulation games](/wiki/Construction_and_management_simulation_games "Construction and management simulation games"), such as *[SimCity](/wiki/SimCity_(1989_video_game) "SimCity (1989 video game)")*, *[Pokémon](/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon_(video_games) "Pokémon (video games)")*, and *[Railroad Tycoon](/wiki/Railroad_Tycoon "Railroad Tycoon")*; as well as among [action](/wiki/Action_game "Action game") and [action-adventure games](/wiki/Action-adventure_game "Action-adventure game"), such as the early *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda "The Legend of Zelda")*, *[Metal Gear](/wiki/Metal_Gear "Metal Gear")*, and *[Grand Theft Auto](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto "Grand Theft Auto")* games.

### Side-scrolling game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Side-scrolling game")]

Main article: [Side-scrolling video game](/wiki/Side-scrolling_video_game "Side-scrolling video game")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Awesomenauts_-_Screenshot_09.jpg/330px-Awesomenauts_-_Screenshot_09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Awesomenauts_-_Screenshot_09.jpg)

*[Awesomenauts](/wiki/Awesomenauts "Awesomenauts")* is a side scrolling [MOBA](/wiki/Multiplayer_online_battle_arena "Multiplayer online battle arena") game.

A **side-scrolling game** or **side-scroller** is a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") in which the viewpoint is taken from the side, and the onscreen characters generally can only move, to the left or right. Games of this type make use of [scrolling](/wiki/Scrolling "Scrolling") computer display technology, and sometimes [parallax scrolling](/wiki/Parallax_scrolling "Parallax scrolling") to suggest added depth.

In many games the screen follows the player character such that the player character is always positioned near the center of the screen. In other games the position of the screen will change according to the player character's movement, such that the player character is off-center and more space is shown in front of the character than behind. Sometimes, the screen will scroll not only forward in the speed and direction of the player character's movement, but also backwards to previously visited parts of a stage. In other games or stages, the screen will only scroll forwards, not backwards, so that once a stage has been passed it can no longer be visited. In games such as [shoot 'em ups](/wiki/Shoot_%27em_up "Shoot 'em up") like *R-type*, the screen scrolls forward by itself at a steady rate, and the player must keep up with the screen, attempting to avoid obstacles and collect things before they pass off screen.

Examples of side-scrolling games include [platform games](/wiki/Platform_game "Platform game") such as *[Sonic the Hedgehog](/wiki/Sonic_the_Hedgehog "Sonic the Hedgehog")* and *[Ori and the Blind Forest](/wiki/Ori_and_the_Blind_Forest "Ori and the Blind Forest")*, [beat 'em ups](/wiki/Beat_%27em_up "Beat 'em up") such as the popular *[Double Dragon](/wiki/Double_Dragon "Double Dragon")* and *[Battletoads](/wiki/Battletoads "Battletoads")*, and [shooters](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game") such as *[R-Type](/wiki/R-Type "R-Type")* and (more recently) *[Jets'n'Guns](/wiki/Jets%27n%27Guns "Jets'n'Guns")*. The *[Super Mario Bros.](/wiki/Super_Mario_Bros. "Super Mario Bros.")* series has used all three types of side-scrolling at some time in its history.

### 2.5D, 3/4 perspective, and pseudo-3D

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2.5D, 3/4 perspective, and pseudo-3D")]

Main article: [2.5D](/wiki/2.5D "2.5D")

See also: [Isometric graphics in video games](/wiki/Isometric_graphics_in_video_games "Isometric graphics in video games") and [Parallax scrolling](/wiki/Parallax_scrolling "Parallax scrolling")

**2.5D** ("two-and-a-half-dimensional"), **3/4 perspective** and **pseudo-3D** are informal terms used to describe graphical projections and techniques that try to "fake" [three-dimensionality](/wiki/Three-dimensional "Three-dimensional"), typically by using some form of parallel projection, wherein the point of view is from a fixed perspective, but also reveals multiple facets of an object. Examples of pseudo-3D techniques include [isometric](/wiki/Isometric_projection "Isometric projection")/[axonometric projection](/wiki/Axonometric_projection "Axonometric projection"), [oblique projection](/wiki/Oblique_projection "Oblique projection"), [orthographic projection](/wiki/Orthographic_projection "Orthographic projection"), [billboarding](/wiki/2.5D#Billboarding "2.5D"), [parallax scrolling](/wiki/Parallax_scrolling "Parallax scrolling"), [scaling](/wiki/2.5D#Scaling_along_the_Z_axis "2.5D"), [skyboxes](/wiki/Skybox_(video_games) "Skybox (video games)"), and skydomes. In addition, 3D graphical techniques such as [bump mapping](/wiki/Bump_mapping "Bump mapping") and [parallax mapping](/wiki/Parallax_mapping "Parallax mapping") are often used to extend the illusion of three-dimensionality without substantially increasing the resulting computational overhead introduced by larger numbers of polygons (also known as the "polygon count").

These terms sometimes possess a second meaning, wherein the gameplay in an otherwise 3D game is forcibly restricted to a two-dimensional plane.

Examples of games that make use of pseudo-3D techniques include *[Zaxxon](/wiki/Zaxxon "Zaxxon")*, *[The Sims](/wiki/The_Sims "The Sims")* and *[Diablo](/wiki/Diablo_(video_game) "Diablo (video game)")* (isometric/axonometric projection); *[Ultima VII](/wiki/Ultima_VII "Ultima VII")* and *[Paperboy](/wiki/Paperboy_(video_game) "Paperboy (video game)")* (oblique projection); *[Sonic the Hedgehog](/wiki/Sonic_the_Hedgehog_(1991_video_game) "Sonic the Hedgehog (1991 video game)")* and *[Street Fighter II](/wiki/Street_Fighter_II "Street Fighter II")* (parallax scrolling); *[Fonz](/wiki/Fonz_(video_game) "Fonz (video game)")* and *[Space Harrier](/wiki/Space_Harrier "Space Harrier")* (scaling); and *[Half-Life 2](/wiki/Half-Life_2 "Half-Life 2")* (skyboxes). In addition to axonometric projection, games such as *[The Sims](/wiki/The_Sims "The Sims")* and *[Final Fantasy Tactics](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_Tactics "Final Fantasy Tactics")* also make use of a combination of pre-drawn 2D sprites and real-time polygonal graphics instead of relying entirely on 2D sprites as is the norm.

## 3D

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 3D")]

See also: [3D computer graphics](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics")

With the advent of 3D [accelerated graphics](/wiki/Graphics_processing_unit "Graphics processing unit"), video games could expand beyond the typically sprite-based 2D graphics of older graphics technologies to describe a view frequently more true to reality and lifelike than their predecessors. Federica Romagnoli has stated that in her opinion, high-budget 3D game graphics "display...levels of artistry once more commonly found in [films](/wiki/Film "Film")"[[3]](#cite_note-3) because of their capability to render complex [cinematography](/wiki/Cinematography "Cinematography") and [CG](/wiki/Computer_animation "Computer animation") characters and the optimization of [video game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") and [PCs](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") to be able to handle such content. [Perspective projection](/wiki/Perspective_projection "Perspective projection") has also been used in some earlier titles to present a 3D view from a fixed (and thus somewhat less hardware-intensive) perspective with a limited ability to move.

### Voxel engines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Voxel engines")]

Instead of using triangle meshes, voxel engines use [voxels](/wiki/Voxel "Voxel").

### Fixed 3D

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Fixed 3D")]

**Fixed 3D** refers to a three-dimensional representation of the game world where foreground objects (i.e. game characters) are typically [rendered](/wiki/Rendering_(computer_graphics) "Rendering (computer graphics)") in [real time](/wiki/Real-time_computing "Real-time computing") against a static background. The principal advantage of this technique is its ability to display a high level of detail on minimal hardware. The main disadvantage is that the player's frame of reference remains fixed at all times, preventing players from examining or moving about the environment from multiple viewpoints.

Backgrounds in fixed 3D games tend to be [pre-rendered](/wiki/Pre-rendered "Pre-rendered") two-dimensional images, but are sometimes rendered in real time (e.g. *[Blade Runner](/wiki/Blade_Runner_(1997_video_game) "Blade Runner (1997 video game)")*). The developers of *[SimCity 4](/wiki/SimCity_4 "SimCity 4")* took advantage of fixed perspective by not texturing the reverse sides of objects (and thereby speeding up rendering) which players could not see anyway.[[4]](#cite_note-sc4d-4) Fixed 3D is also sometimes used to "fake" areas which are inaccessible to players. *[The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda:_Ocarina_of_Time "The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time")*, for instance, is nearly completely 3D, but uses fixed 3D to represent many of the building interiors as well as one entire town (this technique was later dropped in favor of full-3D in the game's successor, *[The Legend of Zelda: Majora's Mask](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda:_Majora%27s_Mask "The Legend of Zelda: Majora's Mask")*). A similar technique, the [skybox](/wiki/Skybox_(video_games) "Skybox (video games)"), is used in many 3D games to represent distant background objects that are not worth rendering in real time.

Used heavily in the [survival horror](/wiki/Survival_horror "Survival horror") genre, fixed 3D was first seen in [Infogrames](/wiki/Infogrames "Infogrames")' *[Alone in the Dark](/wiki/Alone_in_the_Dark "Alone in the Dark")* series in the early 1990s and imitated by titles such as *[Ecstatica](/wiki/Ecstatica "Ecstatica")*. It was later brought back by [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom") in the *[Resident Evil](/wiki/Resident_Evil_(series) "Resident Evil (series)")* series. Gameplay-wise there is little difference between fixed 3D games and their 2D precursors. Players' ability to navigate within a scene still tends to be limited, and interaction with the gameworld remains mostly "point-and-click".

Further examples include the [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation "PlayStation")-era titles in the *[Final Fantasy](/wiki/Final_Fantasy "Final Fantasy")* series ([Square](/wiki/Square_(video_game_company) "Square (video game company)")); the role-playing games *[Parasite Eve](/wiki/Parasite_Eve_(video_game) "Parasite Eve (video game)")* and *[Parasite Eve II](/wiki/Parasite_Eve_II "Parasite Eve II")* ([Square](/wiki/Square_(video_game_company) "Square (video game company)")); the action-adventure games *[Ecstatica](/wiki/Ecstatica "Ecstatica")* and *[Ecstatica 2](/wiki/Ecstatica_2 "Ecstatica 2")* (Andrew Spencer/[Psygnosis](/wiki/Psygnosis "Psygnosis")), as well as *[Little Big Adventure](/wiki/Little_Big_Adventure "Little Big Adventure")* ([Adeline Software International](/wiki/Adeline_Software_International "Adeline Software International")); the graphic adventure *[Grim Fandango](/wiki/Grim_Fandango "Grim Fandango")* ([LucasArts](/wiki/LucasArts "LucasArts")); and *[3D Movie Maker](/wiki/3D_Movie_Maker "3D Movie Maker")* ([Microsoft Kids](/wiki/Microsoft_Kids "Microsoft Kids")).

Pre-rendered backgrounds are also found in some isometric video games, such as the role-playing game *[The Temple of Elemental Evil](/wiki/The_Temple_of_Elemental_Evil_(video_game) "The Temple of Elemental Evil (video game)")* ([Troika Games](/wiki/Troika_Games "Troika Games")) and the *[Baldur's Gate](/wiki/Baldur%27s_Gate "Baldur's Gate")* series ([BioWare](/wiki/BioWare "BioWare")); though in these cases the form of graphical projection used is not different.

### First-person perspective

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: First-person perspective")]

Main article: [First person (video games)](/wiki/First_person_(video_games) "First person (video games)")

[![Screenshot of STALKER: Shadow of Chernobyl](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Stalkershot_2.jpg/250px-Stalkershot_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stalkershot_2.jpg)

First-person perspective as seen in *[STALKER: Shadow of Chernobyl](/wiki/S.T.A.L.K.E.R.:_Shadow_of_Chernobyl "S.T.A.L.K.E.R.: Shadow of Chernobyl")* (2007)

**First person** refers to a [graphical perspective](/wiki/Perspective_(visual) "Perspective (visual)") rendered from the viewpoint of the player character. In many cases, this may be the viewpoint from the cockpit of a vehicle. Many different genres have made use of first-person perspectives, including [adventure games](/wiki/Adventure_game "Adventure game"), [flight simulators](/wiki/Flight_simulator "Flight simulator"), and the highly popular [first-person shooter](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter") genre.

Games with a first-person perspective are usually [avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(computing) "Avatar (computing)")-based, wherein the game displays what the player's avatar would see with the avatar's own eyes. In many games, players cannot see the avatar's body, though they may be able to see the avatar's weapons or hands. This viewpoint is also frequently used to represent the perspective of a driver within a vehicle, as in flight and racing simulators; and it is common to make use of positional audio, where the volume of ambient sounds varies depending on their position with respect to the player's avatar.[[5]](#cite_note-fundamentals-5)

Games with a first-person perspective do not require sophisticated animations for the player's avatar, and do not need to implement a manual or automated camera-control scheme as in third-person perspective.[[5]](#cite_note-fundamentals-5) A first person perspective allows for easier aiming, since there is no representation of the avatar to block the player's view. However, the absence of an avatar can make it difficult to master the timing and distances required to jump between platforms, and may cause [motion sickness](/wiki/Motion_sickness "Motion sickness") in some players.[[5]](#cite_note-fundamentals-5)

Players have come to expect first-person games to accurately scale objects to appropriate sizes. However, key objects such as dropped items or levers may be exaggerated in order to improve their visibility.[[5]](#cite_note-fundamentals-5)

### Third-person perspective

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Third-person perspective")]

Main article: [Third-person (video games)](/wiki/Third-person_(video_games) "Third-person (video games)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Dead_Justice-Cat_Mother-Third-Person-Shooter.jpg/250px-Dead_Justice-Cat_Mother-Third-Person-Shooter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dead_Justice-Cat_Mother-Third-Person-Shooter.jpg)

Screenshot from the [third-person shooter](/wiki/Third-person_shooter "Third-person shooter") *Dead Justice*

**Third person** refers to a [graphical perspective](/wiki/Perspective_(visual) "Perspective (visual)") rendered from a view that is some distance away (usually behind and slightly above) from the player's character.[[5]](#cite_note-fundamentals-5) This viewpoint allows players to see a more strongly characterized avatar, and is most common in [action](/wiki/Action_game "Action game") and [action-adventure games](/wiki/Action-adventure_game "Action-adventure game").[[5]](#cite_note-fundamentals-5) This viewpoint poses some difficulties, however, in that when the player turns or stands with his back to a wall, the camera may jerk or end up in awkward positions. Developers have tried to alleviate this issue by implementing intelligent [camera systems](/wiki/Camera_system "Camera system"), or by giving the player control over the camera.[[5]](#cite_note-fundamentals-5) There are three primary types of third-person camera systems: "fixed camera systems" in which the camera positions are set during the game creation; "tracking camera systems" in which the camera simply follows the player's character; and "interactive camera systems" that are under the player's control.

Examples of games utilizing third-person perspective include *[Super Mario 64](/wiki/Super_Mario_64 "Super Mario 64")*, the *[Tomb Raider](/wiki/Tomb_Raider "Tomb Raider")* series, the 3D installments of the *[Legend of Zelda](/wiki/Legend_of_Zelda "Legend of Zelda")* series, and *[Crash Bandicoot](/wiki/Crash_Bandicoot_(video_game) "Crash Bandicoot (video game)")*.

## Other topics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Other topics")]

### Stereo graphics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Stereo graphics")]

Main article: [Stereoscopic video game](/wiki/Stereoscopic_video_game "Stereoscopic video game")

Stereoscopic video games use [stereoscopic](/wiki/Stereoscopy "Stereoscopy") technologies to create [depth perception](/wiki/Depth_perception "Depth perception") for the player by any form of [stereo display](/wiki/Stereo_display "Stereo display"). Such games are not to be confused with video games that use [3D computer graphics](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics"), which although they feature graphics on screen, do not give the illusion of depth beyond the screen.

### Virtual reality headset

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Virtual reality headset")]

Main article: [Virtual reality headset](/wiki/Virtual_reality_headset "Virtual reality headset")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Sample_screen_capture_of_Oculus_rift_development_kit_2_screen_buffer.jpg/250px-Sample_screen_capture_of_Oculus_rift_development_kit_2_screen_buffer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sample_screen_capture_of_Oculus_rift_development_kit_2_screen_buffer.jpg)

Image captured from Oculus Rift DK2, showing compensation for lens distortion and chromatic aberration

The graphics for virtual reality gaming consist of a special kind of stereo 3D graphics to fit the up-close display. The requirements for [latency](/wiki/Latency_(engineering) "Latency (engineering)") are also higher to reduce the potential for [virtual reality sickness](/wiki/Virtual_reality_sickness "Virtual reality sickness").

### Multi-monitor setup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Multi-monitor setup")]

Main article: [Video gaming on multi-monitor setups](/wiki/Multi-monitor#In_video_gaming "Multi-monitor")

Many games can run **[multi-monitor](/wiki/Multi-monitor "Multi-monitor")** setups to achieve very high [display resolutions](/wiki/Display_resolution "Display resolution"). Running games in this way can create a greater sense of immersion, e.g. when playing a video racing game or flight simulator or give a tactical advantage due to the higher field of view.

### Augmented reality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_graphics&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Augmented reality")]

Main articles: [Augmented reality](/wiki/Augmented_reality "Augmented reality") and [Mixed reality](/wiki/Mixed_reality "Mixed reality")

Augmented reality games typically use 3D graphics on a single flat screen on a smartphone or tablet, or in a head-mounted display. When playing an AR game on a head-mounted device, the visuals are displayed on transparent glass that overlays the real world and has 3D depth through stereoscopic display.
